planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root crowns or container plants in early spring in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; bleeding heart thrives under deciduous trees where it receives spring sun before the leaf canopy closes, then retreats into summer dormancy as conditions dry.

directions: Plant bare-root crowns or container stock in early spring in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part to full shade; set roots 1 inch deep and water thoroughly to establish. Lamprocapnos spectabilis (formerly Dicentra spectabilis) is the quintessential cottage-garden shade perennial, producing arching, 18-to-24-inch stems hung with perfectly heart-shaped, rosy-pink flowers with protruding white inner petals from mid-spring to early summer — the effect of a string of dangling lockets over blue-green, deeply cut foliage has made it one of the most beloved shade-garden plants for over two centuries. Plants go summer-dormant once heat arrives in June or July (earlier in the South), so interplant with hostas, ferns, or astilbes to fill the gap; the dying foliage of bleeding heart can look messy, so grow neighboring plants close enough to cover it naturally. The cultivar 'Gold Heart' has vivid gold foliage for extra shade-garden brightness; 'Alba' has pure white flowers. Hardy in zones 3–9, though in zones 8–9 it blooms earlier (February–April) and goes dormant faster — treat as a spring ephemeral.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Bleeding Heart

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full to part shade; wild ginger slowly colonizes by rhizome and is best established in groups of 3–5 plants set 12 inches apart for quicker coverage.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or fall in rich, moist, well-drained woodland soil in full to part shade; set divisions horizontally just below the soil surface, barely covered, and keep moist until established. Asarum canadense is a native woodland groundcover forming dense, slowly spreading colonies of velvety, kidney-shaped to heart-shaped leaves 4–6 inches wide on fuzzy, 6-to-8-inch petioles, with inconspicuous brownish-red, three-lobed flowers borne at the soil surface in early spring (typically hidden beneath the leaf canopy). The ginger-scented rhizomes were historically used by Indigenous peoples as a flavoring and medicinal plant — the aromatic compound aristolochic acid is present, making internal use inadvisable, but the plant is safe as an ornamental groundcover; the crushed leaves and roots smell intensely of fresh ginger. In deep shade under large deciduous trees where little else grows vigorously, wild ginger thrives and creates a refined, carpet-like effect; it is among the most reliable and handsome native groundcovers for deep-shade situations in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in rich, moist to moderately moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full to part shade; European ginger is slower-spreading than its North American cousin but forms tighter, more refined colonies with glossy evergreen leaves.

directions: Plant container divisions in spring or fall in rich, evenly moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil under deciduous or evergreen trees; set plants slightly shallower than the pot depth and water in well. Asarum europaeum is prized above all other gingers for its exceptionally glossy, deep-green, kidney-shaped leaves — almost succulent-looking — that remain evergreen through winter in zones 5–8, forming tight, slowly spreading mats that look polished even in the darkest garden corners. The small, brownish-purple, bell-shaped flowers appear at soil level in early spring, pollinated by ground-crawling insects. Unlike the deciduous North American wild ginger (A. canadense), European ginger provides four-season interest in woodland gardens, making it especially valuable for shady spots where year-round coverage is needed. It is slower to spread and more expensive than A. canadense but worth the investment for refined shade garden design; space plants 10 inches apart for coverage within 2–3 years. Performs best in zones 5–7; in zones 8–9, it typically goes dormant in summer.

common_name: Miner's Lettuce (Winter Purslane, Spring Beauty Lettuce)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) or very early spring for winter and spring harvest; miner's lettuce self-seeds prolifically and is one of the most cold-tolerant and shade-tolerant cool-season greens, thriving in conditions where other salad plants struggle — it actually prefers partial shade in mild climates, making it ideal under fruit trees or on the north side of structures.

directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface in fall or early spring; press in lightly and keep moist — seeds require light to germinate and should not be covered. Claytonia perfoliata, named for the California Gold Rush miners who relied on it as a vitamin C source to prevent scurvy, is among the most underused winter salad greens for mild-winter climates, producing tender, mild, slightly succulent leaves and distinctive round, dish-shaped flower-leaves (perfoliate bracts encircling the stem) that are the most ornamental and largest leaves on the plant. The flavor is mild and refreshing, similar to watercress without the peppery bite, making it excellent raw in salads or lightly sautéed. In zone 9, it thrives from October through April — harvest leaves when young and tender at 3–4 inches, or cut whole plants at the base for the largest yield. It self-seeds prolifically in moist, shaded spots and can naturalize under fruit trees or in damp corners where it provides reliable winter greens with zero maintenance after the first year.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Miner's Lettuce
Oregon State University Extension — Cool Season Vegetables

common_name: Vietnamese Coriander (Rau Ram, Hot Mint, Laksa Leaf)

planting.note: Propagate by stem cuttings in warm water (roots in 5–7 days) and transplant after last frost in zone 9 (February–March); Vietnamese coriander is a tropical herb that performs exceptionally well in Gulf Coast gardens, thriving in heat and humidity where true cilantro bolts — grow in partial shade to moderate the intense summer heat and extend the harvest.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water and transplant to moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; in zone 9, partial shade is strongly preferred to prevent leaf scorch in summer heat. Persicaria odorata (Vietnamese coriander or rau ram) is the tropical herb that resolves the gardener's dilemma of having fresh coriander flavor through the heat of summer — it produces pointed, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves with distinctive chocolate-brown chevron markings that taste intensely of fresh cilantro with a spicy, peppery undertone; the flavor is actually considered more complex and intense than true coriander by many Southeast Asian cooks. Rau ram is indispensable in Vietnamese cuisine — it is the canonical garnish for pho, rice noodle salads, and laksa soups and is used extensively in Cambodian and Malaysian cooking. In zone 9, it thrives year-round (dying back in rare hard freezes but resprouting from roots), making it far more practical than annual cilantro for consistent harvest. Plants root from stem cuttings placed in water within days — the easiest possible propagation; overwintered plants in pots can be brought indoors when frost threatens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Herbs for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Herbs
Richters Herbs — Vietnamese Coriander Growing Guide
